rsc.li/analyst


# 1. Introduction


Glucose is an important biomolecule and is a source of energy and a metabolic intermediate in living organisms. Maintaining a blood glucose level between3.0mM and8.0mM is essential. An abnormal glucose concentration, particularly in diabetes mellitus, can result in serious complications including kidney failure, heart disease, and blindness. 1The blood glucose test is a common method for diabetes diagnosis, and this test is performed frequently because diabetes patients need to control their glucose intake in their daily diet. However, this test is invasive and painful and brings infection risks to patients. Urine glucose detection is a great alternative to the blood glucose test, because it is non-invasive and safe to patients2,3and can be used to monitor the kidney function. For example, renal glycosuria is diagnosed by measuring the glucose content in urine. 4Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a common oxidizing agent and plays a critical role in food pro-

# Facile synthesis of metal -organic frameworkderived SiW12@Co3O4and its peroxidase-like activity in colorimetric assay †


Wei Shi, a Shuang E, a Meng-Meng Wang, a Tian-Ze Li, a Ting Yang, a Shao-Rong Liu, * b Ming-Li Chen * a and Jian-Hua Wang * a

Over the past few years, artificial enzymes have attracted enormous attention due to their high stabilities and cost-effective productions. In this work, metal -organic framework-derived SiW12@Co3O4was synthesized in large quantities by stirring the mixture at ambient temperature and calcination. The obtained SiW12@Co3O4exhibited a highly inherent peroxidase-like activity and excellent stability. Kinetic studies demonstrated that the synthesized SiW12@Co3O4had a strong binding affinity to3,3 ' ,5,5 ' -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), stronger than HRP had. Specifically, the peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4in an aqueous solution was well maintained after incubation at an elevated temperature, at an extreme pH and for a long time. A SiW12@Co3O4-based method was further developed for H2O2and one-pot glucose detection with good sensitivity and reliability. The facile synthesis approach is expected to facilitate the practical use of metal -organic frameworks and their derivatives as enzyme mimics in the future.

cessing, biomedicine, pharmaceutical, and environment applications. However, H2O2is harmful to human health and life when its concentration exceeds75ppm. Thus, it is necessary to establish a reliable and accurate method to monitor the H2O2concentration. 5 -7H2O2is a product in many enzymecatalyzed reactions. Numerous biological targets such as glucose can be indirectly sensed through H2O2assays. Therefore, H2O2and glucose could be simultaneously detected by a typical horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme-catalyzed reaction.

HRP is a heme-containing enzyme. In a biological process, HRP acts as a catalyst and catalyzes the decomposition of peroxide and hence prevents cells from being damaged by this toxic material. Nevertheless, natural enzymes have several intrinsic defects such as high cost and limited stability and require time-consuming purification steps. 8 -10As alternatives to HRP, peroxidase-like materials have drawn increasing attention in the past few decades. For instance, noble metals, 11 -13metallic oxides, 14 -16and carbon materials17 -19have been discovered to have peroxidase-like properties. Nevertheless, some of these peroxidase-like materials often need laborious preparation procedures and modification processes to avoid aggregation, which can result in batch-to-batch variations, leading to reduced reproducibility. 20,21Furthermore, the colorimetric detection of glucose in an enzyme-catalyzed reaction typically consists of two consecutive steps: GOx catalyzed glucose oxidation and mimetic enzyme-based colorimetric detection of H2O2. Different pH values and temperatures for maximum

Scheme1Schematic illustration of the preparation process of SiW12@Co3O4which is used for H2O2and glucose detection.

activities of GOx and mimetic enzymes in two separate steps often result in complicated operation procedures. 14Thus, developing a simple preparation procedure for a peroxidaselike material and a one-pot glucose sensing method is highly desired.

Metal -organic frameworks (MOFs) are a promising class of hybrid materials constructed from metal clusters/metal ions and organic ligands through coordination bonds. The diverse and fascinating structures of these highly ordered crystalline porous MOFs have afforded abundant emerging functional materials with excellent performance. 22,23For example, MOFs have been demonstrated to be superior sacrificial templates to produce hollow structured materials via calcination under controlled conditions, while the products retain the precursors ' morphologies. 24 -27Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), an attractive subclass of MOFs, are constructed by transition metal ions and2-methylimidazole and can be used as ideal self-sacrificing templates because they can be produced via a facile and economical method. 28 -31

In this work, we introduced negatively charged tungstosilicic acid hydrate (SiW12) into ZIF-67, yielding a SiW12@ZIF-67composite through a one-step method. The SiW12@ZIF-67composite was calcined in an ambient environment, producing dodecahedral shaped SiW12@Co3O4with a lot of negative charges (Scheme1). The SiW12@Co3O4composite exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity, prominent stability, and ultrahigh affinity for TMB compared to HRP. Furthermore, we used SiW12@Co3O4as a peroxidase-like material successfully and developed a simple, sensitive and inexpensive colorimetric method for H2O2and one-pot glucose detection.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1Materials


Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO)3·6H2O), 2-methylimidazole, tungstosilicic acid hydrate (SiW12), glucose, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glucose oxidase (GOx) and Cytochrome C (Cyt C) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Terephthalate (TA), 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH) were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4), anhydrous methanol (CH3OH), and absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were provided by Yongda chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Boric acid (H3BO3) was obtained from Xinhua reagent plant (Shenyang, China). All of the chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification, and deionized water was used throughout this work.


# 2.2Apparatus


Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a LEO1430VP scanning electron microscope (LEO, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a transmission electron microscope (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-6700spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using a D8ADVANCE (BRUKER, Germany) with Cu K α radiation at λ 1.54 Å. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB250XI surface analysis system (Thermo Electron, USA) with an Al/K α excitation source. Zeta potential measurements were carried out using a Malvern Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern, England). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (FL) were monitored on a U-3900UV-Vis spectrophotometer and an F-7000fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan), respectively.


# 2.3Synthesis of materials


SiW12@ZIF-67. SiW12@ZIF-67was synthesized according to the previous literature with slight modification. 32First, 0.722g or2.48mmol of Co(NO)3·6H2O was dissolved in25mL of anhydrous methanol, 100mg of SiW12was dissolved in10mL of deionized water, and the two solutions were mixed and stirred vigorously at room temperature for30min. Then, 1.629g or19.84mmol of2-methylimidazole was dissolved in25mL of anhydrous methanol and quickly poured into the above mixture, forming a purple solution immediately. After the mixture solution was continuously stirred for additional2.5h, the product was centrifuged, and the purple precipitate was collected, washed several times first with water and then with anhydrous methanol to remove the reactants and dried at75 °C overnight.

ZIF-67. The synthesis method of ZIF-67was the same as that of SiW12@ZIF-67except that no SiW12was added.

SiW12@Co3O4and Co3O4. SiW12@ZIF-67or ZIF-67was put into a ceramic crucible and heated to350 °C at a ramp rate of5 °Cmin -1in static air in a muffle furnace. The bluish violet precursor turned into a black powder after3h. After the powder was cooled down slowly to room temperature, the product was washed with deionized water several times and then dried under vacuum at60 °C overnight.


# 2.4SiW12@Co3O4peroxidase-like activity assay


The peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4was investigated through the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the existence of

H2O2. The reaction was performed in2mL of Britton-Robison buffer (BR, 40mM, pH5.0) containing20 µL of2.5M H2O2, 100 µL of10mM TMB in absolute ethanol and40 µL of1mg mL -1SiW12@Co3O4. The maximum absorbance of oxTMB at652nm was measured on a spectrophotometer after30min incubation at room temperature.


# 2.5Kinetics analysis


Kinetic assays were performed in a time-course mode by measuring the absorbance of oxTMB at652nm. The tests were performed by varying either the H2O2concentration at0.5mM TMB or changing the TMB concentration at50mM H2O2. The apparent kinetic parameters were determined via the Lineweaver -Burk plot, 1/ V = Km/ Vmax (1/[S] + 1/ Km), where [S] corresponds to the substrate (H2O2and TMB) concentration, V and Vmax denote the initial reaction velocity and maximal reaction velocity, respectively, and Km is the Michaelis constant.


# 2.6 · OH Detection


· OH was detected by adding TA into the H2O2/SiW12@Co3O4system and measuring the fluorescence spectrum of the resulting solution. 1.0mL of solution containing25mM H2O2, 0.1mM TA, 40mM BR (pH5.0) and a varying concentration of SiW12@Co3O4was prepared and incubated for12h at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum of the resulting solution was recorded at an excitation wavelength of315nm.


# 2.7Cyt C electron transfer


The oxidation of Cyt C was studied by recording the UV-Vis absorption spectrum in1mL of40mM BR buffer (pH5.0) at room temperature. Generally, 50 µL of10mg mL -1reduced Cyt C (red-Cyt C) and50 µL of1mg mL -1SiW12@Co3O4were added into BR buffer, and all of the solutions were deoxygenated. After being fully mixed, the reaction solution was incubated under N2for12h in the dark, and its UV-Vis absorption spectrum was recorded.


# 2.8H2O2and glucose colorimetric detection


For H2O2detection, 20 µL of H2O2with a concentration varying from500 µM to40mM and100 µL of10mM TMB were added into1840 µL of40mM BR buffer (pH5.0). Then, 40 µL of1mg mL -1SiW12@Co3O4was added to start the reaction, and the solution was incubated at37 °C for1h. The absorbance at652nm was measured immediately after the incubation.

For glucose detection, 25 µL of glucose with a concentration varying from200 µM to14mM was mixed with20 µL of5mg mL -1GOx, 25 µL of10mM TMB, 10 µL of1mg mL -1SiW12@Co3O4and420 µL of40mM BR buffer (pH5.0). The mixture was incubated for1.5h at37 °C, and the absorbance at652nm was monitored.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1Synthesis and characterization of SiW12@Co3O4


At step one, ZIF-67and SiW12@ZIF-67templates were synthesized via in situ self-assembly at room temperature as described in the Experimental section. The crystal structures of ZIF-67and SiW12@ZIF-67were determined through XRD measurements. As shown in Fig. S1A, † all XRD peaks of the resultant ZIF-67and SiW12@ZIF-67precursors were consistent with those from the simulated crystal data. No diffraction peaks from impurities were observed. In the following stage, the above synthesized precursors (ZIF-67and SiW12@ZIF-67) were transformed into metal oxide composites through a sacrificial template method. By calcining the ZIF precursors at350 °C for3h, Co3O4and SiW12@Co3O4black powder were obtained. The XRD images of the synthesized Co3O4and SiW12@Co3O4precursors all matched well with that of cubic phase Co3O4 (JCPDS no. 43-1003), and no impurity phases were observed (Fig. S1B † ). Fig. S2 † shows the IR spectra of ZIF-67, SiW12, SiW12@ZIF-67and SiW12@Co3O4. For ZIF-67, the band at422cm -1could be attributed to the Co -N stretch mode, the bands at500 -800cm -1could be attributed to the out of plane vibration of the imidazole ring, the bands in the region of900 -1350cm -1could be ascribed to the plane vibration of the imidazole ring, and the bands at1350 -1500cm -1could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the imidazole ring. 33Compared with ZIF-67, the new absorption peaks of SiW12@ZIF-67at783, 879, 926, and980cm -1could be clearly observed, and these frequencies were attributed to the vibration bands of W -Oc -W, W -Ob -W, Si -Oa, and W v Od, respectively, which are characteristic absorption peaks of SiW12. 34Noticeably, after calcining SiW12@ZIF-67in air, the characteristic absorption peaks of SiW12were still present, but the characteristic absorption peaks of ZIF-67disappeared. At664and574cm -1 , two new absorption peaks appeared, which could be attributed to the Co -O vibration of Co3O4. 35Furthermore, Fig. S3 † shows that Co3O4carried some positive charges. However, after modifying SiW12, the complex carried a lot of negative charges, likely due to the fact that SiW12had many negative charges. 36The above results indicated that ZIF-67and SiW12@ZIF-67precursors were successfully prepared and that Co3O4and SiW12@Co3O4were successfully produced after calcination.

The morphologies of the calcined particles were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. S4A † and Fig. 1A, Co3O4and SiW12@Co3O4retain a typical dodecahedral shape after calcination, with a diameter of approximately500nm and250nm, respectively. TEM images reveal the inner differences before and after adding SiW12. Fig. S4B -D † indicate that the inner structure of Co3O4is hollow. However, after adding SiW12, the inner structure of SiW12@Co3O4is solid (Fig. 1B and C). From Fig. 1D, we can see the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of SiW12@Co3O4, and the lattice spaces in the image of0.285, 0.465, and0.244nm correspond to the (220), (111), and (311) crystal planes of Co3O4. 35Moreover, the elemental mappings

Fig. 1 (A) SEM image of SiW12@Co3O4. (B) and (C) TEM images of SiW12@Co3O4. (D) HRTEM image of SiW12@Co3O4. (E) Element mappings of SiW12@Co3O4.

of SiW12@Co3O4show a uniform spatial distribution of Co, O, Si, and W elements (Fig. 1E). The X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) spectrum shown in Fig. S5 † also confirms the presence of Co, O, Si, and W elements. The above results indicated that SiW12@Co3O4was successfully synthesized.

In order to investigate the surface chemical states of SiW12@Co3O4, the XPS pattern was obtained. The presence of Co, O, C, Si and W elements is shown in the XPS full survey spectrum of SiW12@Co3O4 (Fig. S6 † ). Fig. 2A indicates the Co2p XPS high-resolution spectrum of SiW12@Co3O4. Two distinct peaks at780.6and796.2eV match to the Co2p3/2and Co2p1/2spin -orbit peaks, respectively, with a spin energy separation of15.6eV. A pair of shake-up satellite peaks at786.2and802.8eV is the characteristic of the Co3O4phase. Furthermore, the fitting peaks at781.9and796.7eV are indexed to Co2+ , and the peaks at780.3and795.0eV are assigned to Co3+ . 37The O1s spectrum region shows three well-fitted peaks sited at530.1, 531.2and532.0eV (Fig. 2B), and these peaks correspond to the surface lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies, and chemisorbed oxygen, respectively. 35Besides, the W4f binding energy of SiW12located at35.7and37.8eV is assigned to W(VI), and the peak at102.9eV is indexed to Si2p (Fig. 2C and D). 38,39

Fig. 2High resolution XPS spectra of (A) Co2p, (B) O1s, (C) W4f and (D) Si2p.


# 3.2Peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4


To explore the peroxidase-like activity of the artificial enzyme, chromogenic substrate oxidation catalyzed by SiW12@Co3O4was utilized. The commonly used colorimetric substrates include3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, ε 652nm = 39 000M -1cm -1 ), o -phenylenediamine (OPD, ε 417nm = 16 700M -1cm -1 ) and2,2 ′ -azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6sulfonic acid) (ABTS, ε 420nm = 36 000M -1cm -1 ). Among them, TMB is the preferred chromogenic substrate because it has the highest molar absorptivity and provides the highest sensitivity and because it is relatively stable in solution and safe to human health. 6,40TMB can be oxidized by H2O2, producing a bluish product having the maximum absorption at652nm. As shown in Fig. 3A, SiW12@Co3O4greatly accelerated the TMB oxidation. However, when H2O2or SiW12@Co3O4was present alone, there was little absorption at652nm, and the solution was almost colorless. These results indicated that SiW12@Co3O4had peroxidase properties. When the leaching solution (supernatant of SiW12@Co3O4solution after incubation for2h at room temperature) was used instead of SiW12@Co3O4, the absorption spectrum was virtually the same as that in the absence of SiW12@Co3O4, indicating that free cobalt ions did not possess peroxidase-like activity.

To investigate the mechanism of the SiW12@Co3O4peroxidase-like activity, the catalytic activity of ZIF-67, SiW12, SiW12@ZIF-67, Co3O4and SiW12@Co3O4was measured. As shown in Fig. 3B, Co3O4exhibits some enzyme-like activity, but ZIF-67, SiW12and SiW12@ZIF-67show little activity. These results imply that the peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activity center of SiW12@ Co3O4might derive from Co3O4. The addition of SiW12introduces a great number of negative charges to SiW12@Co3O4, and the negative charges enhance the affinity of SiW12@Co3O4to positively charged TMB.


# 3.3Optimization of assay conditions


The catalytic capability of SiW12@Co3O4, similar to those of HRP and other peroxidase-like materials, is related to the SiW12@Co3O4dosage, pH and temperature of the reaction solution. As shown in Fig. S7, † when the concentration of SiW12@Co3O4is increased, the slope of the corresponding absorbance-time curve increases while the solution background absorbance also rises. In order to obtain relatively high

Fig. 3 (A) UV-Vis spectrum of different reaction solutions. Inset image: photographs of solutions corresponding to spectra. (B) Absorbance at652nm changing with time. All solutions had afinal concentration of20 µg mL -1 .

catalytic activity and comparatively low background, 20 µg mL -1of SiW12@Co3O4was selected as the appropriate concentration to obtain the highest catalytic ability. In Fig. 4A, the peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4and Co3O4was examined by varying the pH from3.0to7.0. The optimum pH of SiW12@Co3O4and Co3O4was5.0and4.0, respectively, indicating that SiW12@Co3O4was more conducive to physiological conditions. Moreover, SiW12@Co3O4held excellent peroxidase-like activity even at pH7.0, and few artificial enzymes including Co3O4could accomplish it at natural pH. This further validated the advantages of introducing SiW12into SiW12@Co3O4. The peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4was also explored by varying the reaction temperature from4to60 °C, and Fig. 4B confirms that the optimal temperature was around37 °C, the physiological temperature. Consequently, for the following analysis of the SiW12@Co3O4activity, a SiW12@Co3O4concentration of20 µg mL -1 , a pH of5.0, and a temperature of37 °C were used.


# 3.4Steady-state kinetics


In order to analyze the peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4, a steady-state kinetics experiment was carried out. Typical Michaelis -Menten curves were obtained within appropriate substrate concentration ranges (Fig. S8A and S8C † ). These data could also be fitted to the Lineweaver -Burk model (Fig. S8B and S8D † ). From the Lineweaver -Burk plot, apparent kinetic parameters were acquired, including the Michaelis constant ( Km) and the maximum reaction velocity ( Vmax). The Km value is related to the affinity of an enzyme to the substrate, with a low Km value representing a high affinity. As seen in Table S1, † the Km value of SiW12@Co3O4with H2O2as the substrate is higher than that of HRP, implying that SiW12@Co3O4has a lower affinity with H2O2than HRP. However, the Km value of SiW12@Co3O4with TMB as the substrate is much lower than that of HRP, indicating a greatly increased affinity between SiW12@Co3O4and TMB compared to that between HRP and TMB. It is noteworthy that SiW12@Co3O4exhibits similar Vmax values for both H2O2and TMB to that of HRP. The above results validated the excellent peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4.


# 3.5Stability of SiW12@Co3O4


Mimetic enzymes are anticipated to be more stable than natural enzymes under harsh conditions. To investigate the

Fig. 4 (A) Effects of pH on SiW12@Co3O4and Co3O4peroxidase-like activity. (B) Effects of temperature on SiW12@Co3O4peroxidase-like activity.

stability of SiW12@Co3O4, the peroxidase-mimicking activity of SiW12@Co3O4was researched after incubation in a wide range of temperatures (4 -80 °C) in various pH (3.0 -11.0) environments for2h (Fig. S9A and S9B † ). SiW12@Co3O4retained most of the peroxidase activity in all temperature and pH ranges. In contrast, HRP lost considerable activity after exposure to temperature above40 °C, and the activity could be observed only after incubation at pH10. 41Moreover, after two months of storage in water under ambient conditions, no remarkable decrease of SiW12@Co3O4activity was detected (Fig. S9C † ). These results confirmed that SiW12@Co3O4possesses outstanding stability and potential for real-world applications.


# 3.6Reactive mechanism


To acquire an insightful understanding of the catalytic reaction mechanism, a sequence of assays were conducted. Typically, the catalytic mechanism of peroxidase-like activity includes the generation of · OH or an electron transfer process. TA is a unique probe molecule capable of capturing · OH yielding highly fluorescent2-hydroxyterephthalic acid. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the amount of · OH generated during the catalytic process. However, as the concentration of SiW12@Co3O4increased, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction solution gradually decreased (Fig. 5A). Therefore, the generation of · OH in the peroxidase-like catalytic process was excluded.

To explore a SiW12@Co3O4-facilitated electron transfer process from TMB to H2O2, red-Cyt C was used as an active electron donor to examine the capacity of SiW12@Co3O4to receive electrons under N2. After adding SiW12@Co3O4, the characteristic absorption peaks of red-Cyt C at520and550nm almost disappeared. A new peak appeared at530nm, which is a typical absorption peak of oxidized cytochrome C (ox-Cyt C) (Fig. 5B). These results indicated that the reaction went through an electron transfer process. First, the abundant negative charges of SiW12@Co3O4facilitated the adsorption of cationic TMB molecules on the surface of SiW12@Co3O4, and TMB donated lone-pair electrons to SiW12@Co3O4. Second, the resulted electron-rich SiW12@Co3O4transferred electrons to adjacent H2O2molecules, which further accelerated the electron transfer process between SiW12@Co3O4and TMB. At the end, TMB was oxidized to blue-colored oxTMB and H2O2was reduced to H2O.

Fig. 5 (A) Fluorescence spectra of TA solutions after incubation with different concentrations of SiW12@Co3O4. (B) UV-Vis spectra of Cyt C incubated with and without SiW12@Co3O4.


# 3.7Detection of H2O2and glucose


Taking advantage of the high intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4, colorimetric experiments for H2O2and glucose detection were carried out. The peroxidase-like activity of SiW12@Co3O4is dependent on the H2O2concentrations, and thus, H2O2detection can be performed via monitoring the absorbance of oxTMB at652nm. Fig. S10A † and Fig. 6A show a good linear relationship ( R2 = 0.995) between5 µM and400 µM H2O2, with a detection limit of1.0 µM. Due to the fact that SiW12@Co3O4 ( pH5.0, 37 °C) and GOx (pH5.0 -7.0, 37 °C) have similar optimal pH and temperature for their catalytic reactions, 42the formation of H2O2catalyzed by GOx and the reduction of H2O2catalyzed by SiW12@Co3O4may be achieved in the same solution. Therefore, a one-pot method for detecting glucose at pH5.0and37 °C was established. Briefly, GOx catalyzed the glucose oxidation first to yield H2O2, and then SiW12@Co3O4catalyzed the TMB oxidation by the newly produced H2O2. Thus, the glucose could be detected by recording the absorbance of oxTMB at652nm. Fig. S10B † and Fig. 6B show that the absorbance intensity exhibits a good linear relationship ( R2 = 0.995) from10 µM to700 µM and a detection limit of3.3 µM glucose. Table S2 † summarizes the analytical performance of the present H2O2and glucose colorimetric sensors, and this SiW12@Co3O4-based method offers improved performance compared to other reported colorimetric methods.

Experiments were conducted to evaluate the method selectivity by replacing glucose with its analogues including sucrose, fructose, lactose and maltose. The absorbance of a glucose analogue solution at a high concentration ( e.g. , 3.5mM) was similar to that of blank solution. The absorbance of a0.7mM glucose solution was many times greater than that of its analogue solutions (Fig. S11 † ). This result suggested that the SiW12@Co3O4-based method has an excellent selectivity for glucose determination.

To confirm the practicability, the proposed method was used for the analysis of complicated samples. As shown in Table S3, † glucose analysis was carried out after a human urine sample (this sample was provided by a healthy volunteer) was100-fold diluted and an orange juice sample (this sample was purchased from the Northeastern University Supermarket) was50-fold diluted. The glucose recoveries were in the range

Fig. 6Linear relationship between the absorbance and concentrations of (A) H2O2and (B) glucose. Inset images of (A) and (B): the photographs of the production of blue-colored products for different concentrations of H2O2and glucose, respectively.

|

of102% -107% with relative standard deviations (RSD) from0.8% to2.8% for both samples, indicating that the proposed method is applicable to determining glucose in real samples.


# 4. Conclusions


In conclusion, SiW12@Co3O4was prepared through a simple sacrificial template method. The obtained SiW12@Co3O4composite had high intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. This new mimetic enzyme exhibited an excellent affinity for TMB, and the affinity toward TMB was considerably greater than that of HRP and most other reported artificial peroxidases. Importantly, SiW12@Co3O4solution retained considerable peroxidase-like activity after long-term storage and being exposed to extreme pH and high temperature. The SiW12@Co3O4composite with peroxidase-like activity was proved to facilitate a simple, sensitive, selective, and low-cost method for the H2O2and one-pot glucose colorimetric detection. Therefore, the sensing method described in the present study holds great promise for practical applications.


# Conflicts of interest


There are no conflicts to declare.
